DigitalProjects

Camila Lívio

Digital data & Visualization

As a part of my philosophy of integrating new media and digital data in my teaching and research, I have created, analyzed and communicated linguistic data to a range of audiences – from foreign language students to the specialized research community. Below are some examples of works that I’ve been doing by taking the world wild web as a corpus.

Language change in Mosquito Coast Spanish (with Madeline Critchfield)

The idea for this project came from my fellow PhD colleague Madeline Critchfield. We compared a particular variety of Spanish to data from Brazilian Portuguese to understand the factors that are predictive of number concord variation. Below you can see the factors that have proven to be significant in motivating such lack of number concord in Brazilian Portuguese (subject/verb word order, presence of intervening material, and high phonic salience of the verb) also are significant predictors in our Mosquito Coast Spanish (MCS) data. Despite differences in the sociohistorical development of these two languages, we propose that variable number concord in MCS is motivated by similar language-internal factors and by the process of incomplete language acquisition.

Stay tuned for the full article in the Proceedings of the 49th LSRL.

Muito (‘very’) vs. Bem (‘well’) in Brazilian Portuguese (with Chad Howe)

You can check out the full article in the Hispania Portuguese Special Issue (December 2020).

Top #10 collocates of muito and bem in the CRPC corpus.

Does bem collocate more frequently with adjectives of positive evaluation than muito?

Women’s day dataset (in Portuguese)

Text mining a Twitter data set collected through the hashtag #diadasmulheres. What are the top #20 adjectives associated with the noun mulheres? Which linguistic hypotheses can we formulate from this initial question?
